expectations.evidence: Transcript references 'designer portfolio' and report title is 'Enhancement Report — 02-portfolio-minimal.html'. The site type is recognized contextually as a portfolio throughout, though no explicit 'site type: portfolio' label is stated.

expectations.text: GSAP and ScrollTrigger scripts are added
expectations.evidence: HTML lines 151-152: GSAP 3.12.5 and ScrollTrigger loaded via CDN. Line 154: gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger). Report section '1. GSAP + ScrollTrigger Integration' confirms this.

expectations.text: Phase A identifies issues: no scroll animations (CRITICAL), no reduced-motion guard (CRITICAL)
expectations.evidence: The enhancement report audit table lists 'No scroll-based reveal animations on any section' as 'High' severity and 'No prefers-reduced-motion consideration' as 'Medium' severity. Neither is labeled 'CRITICAL'. There is no formal 'Phase A' structure — the audit is freeform, not following the expected skill phases.

expectations.text: Phase B adds animations from motion-dev pattern library
expectations.evidence: Animations were added but not from any pattern library. No reference to motion-dev, motion library, catalog.yaml, scores.yaml, or any pattern names from the library. The output uses ad-hoc GSAP code, not library patterns.
No SplitText plugin loaded or used. Grep for 'SplitText' returned zero matches. Hero uses manual .line/.line-inner wrappers with yPercent clip-reveal instead of GSAP SplitText for kinetic typography.

expectations.text: Project items get stagger scroll reveal
expectations.evidence: HTML lines 275-305: gsap.utils.toArray('.project-item').forEach creates per-item ScrollTrigger timelines with staggered title/meta/year reveals. Report section '4. Project Items — Scroll Reveal + Hover Upgrades' confirms choreographed scroll reveal.

HTML lines 310-347: Three separate ScrollTrigger animations for .about-label, .about-text, and .skills-list li with autoAlpha/y reveals. Report section '5. About Section' confirms scroll-triggered entrance.

gsap.matchMedia is NOT used. Instead, window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches is used with a simple if/else block (lines 159-458). The expectation requires gsap.matchMedia() which provides proper GSAP-integrated responsive breakpoints and cleanup, not the native browser API.

expectations.text: Reduced-motion fallback uses clearProps or sets elements to visible state
expectations.evidence: Grep for 'clearProps' and 'visible' returned zero matches in the HTML. The reduced-motion guard simply skips all JS animations (if !prefersReduced wraps everything). Elements using autoAlpha start with visibility:hidden from GSAP's initial state — if animations are skipped, those elements may remain invisible. No clearProps or explicit visible-state fallback is provided.

expectations.text: Summary report follows the specified format with APPLIED/SKIPPED/N-A status per pattern
expectations.evidence: Grep for 'APPLIED', 'SKIPPED', 'N-A', 'N/A' returned zero matches across all output files. The enhancement report uses a freeform narrative format with numbered sections, not the expected structured format with per-pattern status labels.

expectations.text: No CSS transition conflicts with newly added GSAP animations
expectations.evidence: Multiple CSS transitions exist on elements that also receive GSAP animations. For example: .project-title has CSS 'transition: color .4s, transform .4s' but GSAP also animates it with autoAlpha and x transforms (line 292-296). Nav .links a has 'transition: opacity .3s' but GSAP uses autoAlpha (opacity+visibility) on the same elements (lines 204-211). The prefers-reduced-motion CSS sets transition-duration to 0.01ms, but in normal mode these CSS transitions can conflict with GSAP-driven properties.
